title: An Elegant Hairdo, They Say; Fragrant Screw-Pine Flowers, They Say! (4)
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, legal findings, financial figures, witness evidence, and paragraph order. Names, dates, dimensions, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Allegations and findings remain attributed to the judgment and witnesses described in the letter. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The Tamil below is a source-checked reading copy. Page images 123-129 were used to restore the opening salutation, complete a wedding-expense sentence lost at a page break, recover the `70 x 350` marquee dimensions, and repair damaged names and mechanical OCR joins. The source here visibly prints the wedding date as `ஜூலை 7, 1995`; that reading is preserved in this letter. The frozen source file remains unchanged and authoritative. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: You will have read the important observations from Judge Cunha's judgment of the twenty-seventh that I compiled in the previous three letters. Judge Cunha went on to state:

“Jayalalithaa's counsel advanced the defence that Jayalalithaa had given Sasikala power over Jaya Publications and therefore knew nothing of what occurred in that company. This argument cannot be accepted. Whether or not Jayalalithaa gave Sasikala power of attorney, the unalterable fact is that Jayalalithaa and Sasikala were partners with equal authority in Jaya Publications.

“The power Jayalalithaa gave Sasikala enabled Sasikala to administer Jaya Publications independently and thereby remain its indirect owner. Jayalalithaa appointed Sasikala as her agent. Yet every transaction conducted through the company took place only with Jayalalithaa's approval, as the prosecution evidence establishes. Neither did Sasikala conduct Jaya Publications' transactions without Jayalalithaa's knowledge, nor did Jayalalithaa conduct them without Sasikala's participation. Both knew of all money received by the company and transferred from it to other bank accounts.

“Jayalalithaa's claim that she knew nothing of such activities by Sasikala, Sudhakaran and Ilavarasi, who lived in her house, cannot be accepted. How can the Chief Minister of a State claim to know nothing about the conduct of associates living in her own home? Nor could Jayalalithaa deny that all three lived there. The electoral roll gives 36 Poes Garden as the address of Sasikala, Sudhakaran and Ilavarasi.

“When Jayalalithaa was examined and asked why Sasikala and Ilavarasi lived there, she replied that she did not wish to answer. She did not say that they had never lived with her. Since neither Sasikala nor Ilavarasi was related to Jayalalithaa by blood or otherwise, Jayalalithaa could not explain clearly why they lived with her.

“Sasikala and Ilavarasi did not answer that question either. Both had separate families and were married. Why should they leave their families and live with Jayalalithaa in the same house for so many years? The gifts received by Jayalalithaa and the money earned improperly by using her name all came through Sasikala. Ilavarasi was used to bring them to Jayalalithaa's house.

“Jayalalithaa cannot claim ignorance of the companies run by Sasikala and Ilavarasi. She gave Rs. 1 crore to Sasi Enterprises. She also issued surety cheques in her own name for all bank loans taken by their companies. This shows that they acted jointly, fully aware of what they were doing. It has been properly established that Jayalalithaa, Sasikala, Sudhakaran and Ilavarasi could not account for how the properties in their names were acquired.”
Judge Cunha explained this in his judgment.

It was not only assets accumulated beyond known income but also expenditure beyond known income that brought consequences upon Jayalalithaa. The extravagant marriage of her then foster son Sudhakaran stands as a fundamental example of such expenditure in the Bengaluru disproportionate-assets case.

The Tamil Nadu Directorate of Vigilance and Anti-Corruption submitted extensive evidence about how Jayalalithaa conducted Sudhakaran's lavish wedding: receipts, accounts filed with the Income Tax Department, witness testimony and valuations by Public Works Department officials. Jayalalithaa's side had no evidence with which to deny them. Let us see how Judge Cunha described the matter:

“Jayalalithaa's foster son Sudhakaran married Sathyalakshmi, the granddaughter through his daughter Shanthi of actor Sivaji Ganesan, on 7 September 1995. The marquees cost Rs. 5,21,23,532; food, water and ceremonial betel offerings cost Rs. 1,14,96,125; silver plates cost Rs. 4 lakh; and postage for the invitations cost Rs. 2,24,000. Witness testimony established every expenditure incurred for this wedding.

“The 181st witness in the case was Thangarajan, an Assistant Engineer in the Public Works Department who valued Sudhakaran's wedding expenses. The evidence was gathered by meeting architect Vijayashankar, who designed the marquees; art director Thotta Tharani; Gopinath; Thotta Tharani's assistants Ramesh and Srinivasan; and electricians P.S. Mani and Syed Mohammed, who arranged the lighting. The valuation used the prevailing market prices at the time of the wedding. On that basis, they calculated Sudhakaran's wedding expenditure at Rs. 5.91 crore.”

Jayalalithaa's counsel P. Kumar argued against these witnesses: “These estimates, prepared two and a half years after Sudhakaran's wedding, cannot be accepted. The witnesses who prepared them did not attend the wedding. How could they understand the structures and marquees that had been erected there? Their testimony should therefore be rejected.”

Senior Public Works Department Engineer Muthusamy was examined as a prosecution witness. He supervised marquee construction for government functions. Jawahar Babu, an IAS officer then serving as an Assistant Secretary to Chief Minister Jayalalithaa, telephoned Muthusamy and said: “We need to speak with you about a wedding to be held at the Chief Minister's residence. Come to the Poes Garden house.”

Muthusamy met Jawahar Babu the next day and was introduced to Sasikala. Sasikala told him: “You must oversee all the work of erecting marquees for Sudhakaran's wedding.” According to Muthusamy, Vijayashankar prepared the plans.

“Five principal marquees measuring 70 by 350 feet were erected at MRC Nagar by Rajappa Nadar of Thoothukudi. Eight coconut-leaf marquees measuring 60 by 450 feet were constructed. Rajagopal of Mannargudi erected the VIP dining marquee, measuring 60 by 200 feet. Kumaresan Nadar erected kitchen marquees measuring 45 by 1,356 feet. Ethiraj provided the wedding stage, air conditioners for the bride and groom, their retiring rooms and toilets. He stated that Jayalalithaa's family bore all these expenses.

“Art director Gopinath arranged the VIP seating, its decorative work and dining areas. Balthasan installed five borewells for the water supply, while water was also supplied by lorries. C.S. Chandrasekaran provided the electricity connection, using two ten-kilowatt generators and four mobile generators. Muthusamy testified that Jayalalithaa and Sasikala supervised the work while it was underway.”
After deducting the amount paid by the bride's father, Narayanasamy, Jayalalithaa paid the entire balance, issuing cheques in her own name. Bank records proved all of them.

A cheque for Rs. 1.5 lakh was issued from Poes Garden to Pal Babu, a North Indian decoration specialist. Jayalalithaa also issued cheques in her own name to Subramani and Sami, who improved the road from the Adyar Vinayagar Temple to MRC Nagar and decorated both sides with lights.

Thus, after questioning Muthusamy, the senior Public Works Department engineer who supervised every wedding arrangement from the marquees onward, Engineer Thangaraj assessed the marquee and other costs. He used the actual market prices of those materials in 1995, the year of the wedding.

When Jayalalithaa appeared and testified about Sudhakaran's wedding, she claimed that she had spent nothing and had made this clear in her response to the Income Tax Department. Yet in her income-tax return for 1996-97, she recorded Rs. 25,98,521 as wedding expenses and Rs. 3,94,240 as cash expenditure. Documents seized from Jayalalithaa's auditor's house also show that the accounts submitted to the Income Tax Department clearly recorded Rs. 12,00,050 as expenditure on wedding notices and advertisements.

If the bride's family bore every wedding expense, why did Jayalalithaa sign and issue cheques for the marquees, lighting, vehicles and invitations? Judge Cunha stated that Jayalalithaa's side could not answer these questions. Many who carried out the wedding work testified in the case. I do not wish to recount every one of them.

Udanpirappē, this series titled “An Elegant Hairdo, They Say; Fragrant Screw-Pine Flowers, They Say!” has now continued for four days. The judgment of the Special Judge in Bengaluru is more extensive still. As I said at the outset, I had not wished to comment on it. But it was the ruling AIADMK and certain newspapers that prompted me to do so.

On the day the Supreme Court granted Jayalalithaa conditional bail, advertisements in the ruling party's official daily propagated the idea that the Court had somehow delivered a wrongful judgment and that a false case had been filed against Jayalalithaa. It was to clarify certain truths in response that I wrote this detailed series.

Take today's letter alone. Jayalalithaa repeatedly claimed that she knew nothing of what happened in Jaya Publications; that she knew nothing of the activities of Sasikala, Sudhakaran and Ilavarasi, though they lived in her home; that she knew nothing of the companies run by Sasikala and Ilavarasi; and that she spent nothing on Sudhakaran's wedding. Judge Cunha's judgment disproved each of these successive falsehoods with evidence.
I wished only to bring these matters into the open. As I have already said, I again make clear that my purpose is not to wound anyone.
With affection,
M.K.
24 October 2014
